summary: UR261 (KH0497, Proyecto Incahuasi, Sector C UE 2, Big Pile) is a 15-cord delivery tag physically tied onto another khipu's primary cord via a knot and 10 cm loop. Only 6 numerical cords carry values, organised as three sub-groups separated by empty cords (30 | 35+55 | 10+73+116, sum=319), followed by a single STRING cord reading chaqa (delivery / transfer across boundary in commodity context). No KFG pendant-pendant sum page exists for this khipu (no Ascher lattice). The document is interpreted as a subsidiary delivery ticket, a tag attached to a parent khipu in the same Big Pile as UR260 (aji micro-audit) and UR256 (aji audit). v1 to v3 delta: 5 KFG-resolved cords among 8 INT (63% of numerical content previously unreadable); without this resolution, the tag's three-group structure could not be reconstructed.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  15 cords: 12 L1 + 3 L2. 8 INT, 1 STRING, 6 EMPTY. Architecture 1-PAIRE. Schema RELATIONAL (depth L2). Clusters = 2 (C1 of 11 cords, C2 of 1 empty cord). Provenance: Incahuasi, Sector C, Subsector 5, Unidad 19K, UE 2. Museum: Proyecto Incahuasi. KFG twin: KH0497 (no pendant-pendant sum catalog page; Ascher layer unavailable).
  Physical context (OKR field notes): 'Big Pile' find, same archaeological assemblage as UR260. CRITICAL physical detail: 'Knot and 10 cm loop around larger primary cord at 3 cm.' UR261 was physically TIED ONTO another khipu's primary cord -- this is an attached tag, not a standalone document. The 10 cm loop + knot attachment is the signature of a subsidiary delivery ticket in the Incahuasi archive.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  OKR/KFG agreement: 14% raw (1 AGREED + 1 DIVERGENT + 5 KFG_RESOLVED + 8 OKR_ONLY L2s). The low raw agreement figure reflects v1 gaps, not source conflict: 5 of the 8 INT cords had NULL turns in OKR v1 and were filled by KFG. 1 DIVERGENT cord (7% of twinned set, under the 10% warning threshold).
  v1 to v3 delta: 5 KFG-resolved cords. Without merged-corpus resolution, v1 would have shown only 3 of 8 numerical values and could not have reconstructed the three-group tag structure.

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. KFG catalog does not publish pendant-pendant sums for KH0497 (404 on https://www.khipufieldguide.com/notebook/fieldmarks/pendant_pendant_sum/html/KH0497.html). The document is too small or too simple for an audit lattice. No capacity-class computation, no covering_ratio, no cascade depth. This absence is consistent with the interpretation: a delivery tag is a low-integrity signal-carrier attached to a parent, not itself an audit-grade artefact.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  C1 (p1-p11, tot=319): the data block. AB dominant, with one MB cord at p9 (val=73). Three sub-groups separated by empty cords:
    Group A: p1 (val=30)
    [p2, p3 EMPTY]
    Group B: p4 (val=35), p5 (val=55)
    [p6, p7 EMPTY]
    Group C: p8 (val=10), p9 (val=73, MB), p10 (val=116)
    Label: p11 (STRING chaqa)
  C2 (p12, tot=0): a single EMPTY cord acting as document closure.
  Sum verification: 30 + 35 + 55 + 10 + 73 + 116 = 319 = C1 declared total. Arithmetic consistent.
  The three-group partition (1 | 2 | 3 cords, values 30 | 90 | 199) is the tag's own internal segmentation. No canonical interpretation pins which group means what (by-category, by-date, by-supplier); see the HYPOTHESIS section for the preferred reading.

VOCABULARY (1 unique, 100% hits on 1 STRING cord)
  chaqa x1 -- p11 C1 AB -- delivery / transfer across boundary (commodity context). The cha->na rule (chaqa -> naqa = slaughter) is SUPPRESSED: no juridical context, no violence vocabulary, no kinship words. In a storehouse ticket physically tied to another khipu, chaqa carries its commodity reading: 'delivery.'

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p11 C1 AB STRING chaqa (closing label of the data block)

COLOR ROLE MAP
  AB (14/15 cords): natural undyed -- the default pastoral/commodity register. Dominant here because the tag inherits the document-class colour of the parent khipu family.
  MB (1 cord, p9, val=73): single non-AB cord inside Group C. The only colour distinction -- likely a commodity-category sub-marker, possibly the only entry representing a different commodity class within this delivery.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR261 is a delivery tag physically tied onto another khipu from the same Sector C UE 2 Big Pile. The tag records three sub-groups of quantities totalling 319 units, closed by a single word chaqa ('delivery'). It is the functional equivalent of a paper bill-of-lading pinned to a ledger entry: the parent khipu records the ledger, UR261 is the slip attached to the delivery itself.
  The three sub-groups plausibly encode: (Group A = initial quota or carry-over = 30), (Group B = two supplier lots = 35 + 55 = 90), (Group C = three delivery entries = 10 + 73 + 116 = 199). The lone value 10 at p8 is the Urton-Chu aji positional constant; combined with co-location next to the known aji audit UR256 in the same UE, the tag is likely part of the aji documentary stream.
  Alternative: the document could be a simple record slip for any commodity, with chaqa functioning as a generic delivery label. The commitment to 'aji' rests on the co-location with UR256 and the lone 10-value; the reading should be taken as hypothesis-level, not proven.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Group A (p1 = 30): first entry. If quota: 'thirty allocated.' If carry-over: 'thirty from prior.'
  [p2, p3 EMPTY]: first phase separator.
  Group B (p4 = 35, p5 = 55): two entries. Possibly two supplier lots, two delivery waves, or two sub-categories summing to 90.
  [p6, p7 EMPTY]: second phase separator.
  Group C (p8 = 10, p9 = 73 on MB, p10 = 116): three entries, with p9 marked in a distinct colour. The final entry (116) is the largest single value.
  Label (p11 chaqa): 'delivery.' Closes the tag.
  p12 EMPTY: terminal padding, or the attachment-point marker where the tag was tied.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Inca storehouses (qollqas) of the Inkawasi type recorded deliveries with a hierarchy of documents: the primary khipu (the ledger), audit khipus that verified the ledger (e.g. UR260, UR256), and small delivery tags attached at the point of deposit. Colonial sources (Murua, Cobo) describe kamayoq scribes managing these hierarchies on each arrival at the storehouse. The physical 'knot and 10 cm loop' attachment on UR261 is the material signature of that tag-to-parent relationship. Urton & Chu (2015) implicitly acknowledge this stratified archive at Inkawasi but the attached-tag micro-document class is under-described in the published literature.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 (val=30): opening entry.
  p8 (val=10, AB): the lone 10 -- the Urton-Chu aji positional constant, weak but suggestive co-occurrence signal.
  p9 (val=73, MB): the single colour-marked data cord -- a distinguished entry, possibly a different commodity class or an adjustment.
  p11 (chaqa, AB STRING): the only word -- 'delivery' -- the tag's label.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  - Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi field records for Sector C UE 2: does the excavation photograph or field note identify which larger khipu UR261 was tied to? That parent khipu (UR255, UR256, UR257, UR258, UR259, UR260 are all candidates) would confirm or refute the delivery-tag interpretation.
  - Cross-khipu arithmetic: does one of the co-located khipus contain 319 as a recorded total, or individual entries matching 30 / 90 / 199?
  - KFG catalog page https://www.khipufieldguide.com/catalog/KH0497.html for photos of the knot-and-loop attachment.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  v2 corpus annotation classifies UR261 as INDETERMINE, 0% hit rate, 0 vocabulary, noting 6 empty cords ('beaucoup_vides'). v3 gives 100% hit rate on the 1 STRING cord, resolves 5 of 8 INT cords, and establishes the three-group tag structure with the chaqa delivery label.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - Architecture 1-PAIRE (preserved).
  - 6 empty cords noted as a distinctive feature (preserved and interpreted as phase separators).
  - Very small khipu size (preserved, reinterpreted as 'tag' rather than 'minor/broken document').
narrative:
A delivery. That is all the khipu says in words -- one cord, one label, chaqa. Everything else is three small groups of numbers, organised with the economy of a receipt.

The tag opens with a single value of thirty on cord 1, alone, separated from what follows by two empty cords. Then two entries, thirty-five and fifty-five, on cords 4 and 5 -- another pair of empties, and then three more numbers: a ten, a seventy-three, a hundred-and-sixteen. Add them all up and you get three hundred and nineteen. The scribe did not write that total anywhere -- the tag is too small for a summary cord. He let the empty separators do the bookkeeping, each blank interval marking the boundary between one batch and the next.

On cord 11, the only word: chaqa. In a juridical context the cha-to-na rule would make this naqa, slaughter -- but there is no juridical context here. No tata, no kaka, no taka, no violence. This is a storehouse pile at Sector C UE 2 at Incahuasi, the same archaeological assemblage that holds UR260 (the aji micro-audit) and UR256 (the full aji audit), and in that context chaqa means what a colonial auditor would have written as entrega: the delivery crossed the boundary from the supplying ayllu to the state store.

The physical artefact tells the rest of the story. The field notes describe it plainly: a knot, a ten-centimetre loop, tied around a larger primary cord at three centimetres. UR261 is not a standalone khipu. It was tied onto another document -- pinned to it, literally, the way a clerk pins a receipt to a ledger page. A tag. A delivery slip. The parent khipu records the general ledger; this little tag records the specific transaction, fastened to the entry it corresponds to, ready to travel with the merchandise if the tie ever came loose.

The one hint of category comes at cord 9: seventy-three, in medium-brown rather than the natural undyed fibre of its neighbours. Inside Group C, one of the three delivery entries is marked differently -- perhaps a different commodity class within the same delivery, perhaps an adjustment, perhaps an entry attributable to a different supplier. We cannot say from fifteen cords alone, only note that the scribe made the distinction deliberately.

And the lone ten at cord 8, hovering between the aji positional constant from Urton and Chu and ordinary coincidence, gives the best weak signal this small document can offer. Ten by itself proves nothing; ten in a pile where UR256 is the aji audit makes it more likely than not that this tag belongs to that stream -- a slip for one aji delivery, three hundred and nineteen units in three batches, tied to its place in the ledger and sealed by the single word chaqa.

prior_work.claim: Incahuasi Sector C UE 2 'Big Pile' contains a cluster of khipus recovered together by the Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi excavation (UR255, UR256, UR257, UR258, UR259, UR260, UR261 inclusive).
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository field-note records; Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015), Accounting in the King's Storehouse: The Inkawasi Khipu Archive, Ethnohistory 62(1).

prior_work.claim: Positional constants 15 and 10 mark aji (chili) audit khipus at Incahuasi; 47 and 208 mark peanut khipus.
Inca storehouse archives are stratified: primary ledger khipus, audit khipus that verify ledgers, and delivery / receipt slips attached at deposit time.
prior_work.citation: Martin de Murua (1611), Historia General del Peru; Bernabe Cobo (1653), Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

v3_contribution.claim: UR261 reclassified from v2 INDETERMINE (0% hit rate, 0 vocabulary) to commodity_accounting / delivery_tag sub-type, based on the single chaqa label in commodity context, the three-group internal structure segmented by empty cords, and the physical knot-and-loop attachment to a parent primary cord.
v3 merged OKR*KFG corpus resolves 5 of the 8 numerical L1 cords (63% of the data) previously NULL in v1 OKR. Without this resolution, the three-group internal structure could not be reconstructed.

evidence.test: Count of KFG_RESOLVED cords in merged corpus quality distribution

v3_contribution.claim: UR261 is a physically attached tag: the field notes document a knot and 10 cm loop tied around a larger primary cord at 3 cm. This is the first deep-read documentation in the ALBA corpus of a khipu functioning explicitly as an attached delivery ticket rather than a standalone ledger.

evidence.test: OKR field note: 'Knot and 10 cm loop around larger primary cord at 3 cm'

v3_contribution.claim: The three-group internal segmentation (1 cord | 2 cords | 3 cords, values 30 | 90 | 199, total 319) is a size-progressive delivery-batch pattern. The lone value 10 at p8 is consistent with the Urton-Chu aji positional constant but not conclusive alone.

evidence.test: Empty-cord phase separation count = 3 internal groups

v3_contribution.claim: KFG catalog does not publish pendant-pendant sums for KH0497 (404 on the Ascher page). The absence of an Ascher lattice is consistent with the delivery-tag interpretation -- tags do not require audit-grade integrity.

companions.note: Co-located in the same Sector C UE 2 Big Pile. UR260 is the micro-audit khipu (47 cords, 12+12 structure, 3/3 Ascher verified). UR261 is likely a delivery tag for one of the transactions recorded in UR260 or an adjacent ledger.
UR256 is the known aji audit khipu from Sector C UE 2 Big Pile. The most likely parent khipu to which UR261 was physically tied, given the size differential (UR256 has 176 cords vs UR261's 15).

evidence.test: Identical find-spot; primary cord of UR256 is a candidate for the 'larger primary cord' mentioned in UR261 field notes

companions.note: UR255 (90 cords, Incahuasi) is another candidate parent khipu from the same pile.
UR257 (14 cords, Incahuasi) is a similarly-small companion document; worth testing whether UR257 and UR261 together form a ticket set attached to the same parent.
UR258 (48 cords, Incahuasi) is a close structural match in size and architecture.
UR259 (15 cords, Incahuasi) is the same size as UR261 and may be another attached tag from the same parent.

word_reading.note: 1 STRING cord, 1/1 dictionary match (100%). No Pass 3A or Pass 3D readings because no Ascher lattice exists for this khipu. The single word chaqa is read in commodity context (delivery) rather than juridical context (slaughter via cha->na rule), as mandated by the storehouse provenance and the absence of violence/kinship vocabulary.

document_type.note: Strong signals: Incahuasi Sector C UE 2 Big Pile provenance, physical attachment to a parent primary cord (knot + 10 cm loop), small 15-cord size, chaqa as delivery label. Weak signals: only one STRING cord, no Urton-Chu constant density (only one value=10 at p8), no Ascher lattice for structural confirmation. The 'delivery tag' sub-type is supported by the physical evidence and the co-location but remains a hypothesis because no audit-grade signature is present.

narrative.note: The delivery-tag hypothesis is internally coherent and physically attested by the field-note description of the attachment. The three-group size-progressive structure (30 | 90 | 199) is a plausible delivery-batch pattern. Identification of the specific parent khipu (UR256 is the leading candidate) is not yet confirmed by field records -- this remains the weakest link in the narrative.

data_integrity.note: Only 1 AGREED cord in OKR/KFG overlap; 5 KFG_RESOLVED and 1 DIVERGENT. DIVERGENT fraction 7% (under the 10% warning threshold). No Ascher lattice available. Arithmetic consistency: sum of the six numerical cords = 319, matching the C1 declared total. Physical damage: none noted. The document is small and straightforward but with no audit lattice to cross-verify.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). The Sky/Ground Polysemy Rule in Inca Khipu Vocabulary. ALBA Project preprint.
Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu. Dover Publications.
Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015). Accounting in the King's Storehouse: The Inkawasi Khipu Archive. Ethnohistory 62(1).
Murua, Martin de (1611). Historia General del Peru.
Cobo, Bernabe (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo.
Gonzalez Holguin, Diego (1608). Vocabulario de la Lengua General de Todo el Peru llamada Lengua Qquichua.
